lines: Find a comfortable sitting position. [long pause] This practice is traditionally done sitting, with both feet on the floor and hands resting in your lap or on your knees. [long pause] But if lying down is what your body needs today, that's fine.
The important thing is that your spine is as straight as comfortable. [long pause] Not rigid. [long pause] Just... [long pause] [long pause] upright. [long pause] Alert but at ease.
Close your eyes. [long pause] Let your shoulders fall. [long pause] Release your jaw. [long pause] Let your hands go soft.
Take a slow breath in... [long pause] [long pause] and let it go.
Again. [long pause] In... [long pause] [long pause] and release.
Let the breathing settle into its natural rhythm. [long pause] You don't need to guide it from here.

We are going to practice centering prayer. [long pause] This is one of the oldest forms of Christian contemplation — rooted in the Desert Fathers and Mothers of the fourth century, refined by the author of The Cloud of Unknowing in the fourteenth, and revived in our time by Thomas Keating and Basil Pennington.
It is, in essence, very simple. [long pause] You sit in God's presence. [long pause] And you consent to be there.

There is no insight to achieve. [long pause] No emotion to manufacture. [long pause] No problem to solve. [long pause] This is not thinking about God. [long pause] It is being with God. [long pause] The way you might sit with an old friend in comfortable silence — not because there's nothing to say, but because the relationship has gone deeper than words.
The psalmist writes: "Be still, and know that I am God." Psalm forty-six, verse ten.
Be still. [long pause] Not "be productive." Not "be insightful." Be still.
Here is how the practice works.

First, choose a sacred word. [long pause] This word is not a mantra. [long pause] You're not repeating it to create an effect. [long pause] It's a symbol — a gesture of your intention to consent to God's presence and action within you.

The word should be short. [long pause] One or two syllables. [long pause] It should resonate with your desire for God. [long pause] Traditional choices include: Jesus. [long pause] Abba. [long pause] Come. [long pause] Peace. [long pause] Mercy. [long pause] Love. [long pause] Stillness. [long pause] Here.
Take a moment now. [long pause] Let a word rise. [long pause] Don't overthink it. [long pause] The word that comes... [long pause] [long pause] is fine.
Good. [long pause] Hold that word lightly. [long pause] Like a feather in an open palm.
Now, here's the practice. [long pause] I'll explain it simply, and then we'll enter the silence together.
You sit with eyes closed. [long pause] You introduce your sacred word gently — not as a repetition, but as a way of saying to God: "I am here. [long pause] I consent to your presence."

Thoughts will come. [long pause] They always come. [long pause] Sensations, memories, plans, emotions, itches, fantasies, worries, brilliant ideas, grocery lists... [long pause] [long pause] they will all come. [long pause] This is normal. [long pause] This is not failure. [long pause] This is being human.

When you notice you've been carried away by a thought — and you will notice, because something in you is deeper than the thoughts — you gently return to your sacred word. [long pause] Not with frustration. [long pause] Not with self-judgment. [long pause] Just... [long pause] [long pause] gently. [long pause] The way you'd pick up a child who's wandered off. [long pause] No scolding. [long pause] Just a gentle return.

Thomas Keating described it this way: thoughts in centering prayer are like boats going by on a river. [long pause] You're sitting on the bank. [long pause] The boats will pass. [long pause] You don't need to get on any of them. [long pause] And when you realize you've somehow ended up on a boat — you simply step back onto the bank.
Your sacred word is how you step back onto the bank.
That's all there is. [long pause] Consent. [long pause] Silence. [long pause] Thoughts. [long pause] Return. [long pause] Consent again.

The "goal" — if there is one — is not the absence of thought. [long pause] It is the willingness to return. [long pause] Every time you return to your sacred word, you are practicing surrender. [long pause] You are practicing trust. [long pause] You are saying, again and again: I choose you over my own noise.

Elijah knew this. [long pause] In First Kings, chapter nineteen, he stood on the mountain waiting for God. [long pause] And a great wind came — but God was not in the wind. [long pause] And an earthquake came — but God was not in the earthquake. [long pause] And a fire came — but God was not in the fire.
And then... [long pause] [long pause] a still small voice. [long pause] Some translations say "a sound of sheer silence."

The wind, the earthquake, the fire — those are your thoughts. [long pause] Dramatic. [long pause] Compelling. [long pause] Hard to ignore. [long pause] But God is not in them. [long pause] God is in what comes after. [long pause] In the quiet beneath the noise.
You don't have to fight the wind. [long pause] You just have to wait for the silence.

Let's begin. [long pause] I'm going to guide you into the silence, and then I will step away. [long pause] You'll hear my voice a few times — gently — to help you return if you've drifted. [long pause] But mostly, this is between you and God.
Settle into your body. [long pause] Feel the chair beneath you. [long pause] Feel the air around you.
Gently introduce your sacred word. [long pause] Not spoken aloud. [long pause] Just a whisper in your heart. [long pause] A breath of consent.
And now... [long pause] [long pause] be still.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
If you've been carried away by a thought... [long pause] [long pause] that's okay. [long pause] Gently return to your sacred word. [long pause] No judgment. [long pause] Just a quiet return.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
You're here. [long pause] That's all that's asked. [long pause] Every return to the word is an act of love.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

If your mind is busy... [long pause] [long pause] that's fine. [long pause] Let the thoughts come and go. [long pause] You don't have to stop them. [long pause] Just don't serve them tea. [long pause] Return gently to your sacred word.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
Be still... [long pause] [long pause] and know.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
Gently... [long pause] [long pause] your sacred word. [long pause] A quiet return.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
Rest in the silence. [long pause] God is here. [long pause] You don't have to find him. [long pause] He's already found you.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
We're nearing the end of our time in silence. [long pause] Don't rush out. [long pause] Stay where you are for another moment.
Now... [long pause] [long pause] with your eyes still closed... [long pause] [long pause] begin to let ordinary awareness return. [long pause] But slowly. [long pause] Like waking from a deep rest.
Feel your hands. [long pause] Your feet. [long pause] The weight of your body.
Let me close with a prayer that the Christian contemplative tradition has used for centuries to end centering prayer.

Our Father, who art in heaven, hallowed be thy name. [long pause] Thy kingdom come, thy will be done, on earth as it is in heaven. [long pause] Give us this day our daily bread, and forgive us our trespasses, as we forgive those who trespass against us. [long pause] And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil. [long pause] For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, forever and ever.
Open your eyes when you're ready. [long pause] Slowly. [long pause] Let the light in gradually.

Whatever happened during that silence — whether it felt deep or distracted, peaceful or restless — it was enough. [long pause] Centering prayer is not measured by experience. [long pause] It's measured by consent. [long pause] And you consented. [long pause] You showed up. [long pause] You sat in the silence with God.
Thomas Keating said: "The only failure in centering prayer is not to do it."

If this practice resonated with you, return to it. [long pause] Twenty minutes, twice a day, is the traditional recommendation. [long pause] But even five minutes of sitting in willing silence before God is a radical act in a world that never stops talking.
Go gently. [long pause] Carry the silence with you.
